slide.title: DALI2
slide.subtitle:
Multi-Agent System Framework Built on SWI-Prolog Process-per-Agent Architecture · Redis Star Topology · Full DALI Compatibility
University of L'Aquila — DISIM

bullets.b: **Introduction** — What is DALI? What is DALI2?
**Architecture** — Redis star topology, process-per-agent model
**Language Fundamentals** — Agent declarations, suffixes, operators
**Reactive Rules** — External events, actions, communication
**Internal Events and Periodic Tasks** — Proactive behavior
**Condition and Environment Rules** — Monitors, constraints
**Goals and Memory** — Achieve/test goals, past events, export past
**Communication** — Tell/told filtering, FIPA messages, proposals
**Advanced Features** — Ontology, learning, AI Oracle, blackboard
**Agent Lifecycle** — Cycle-based event loop
**Examples** — Agriculture, emergency, showcase, distributed

slide.title: What is DALI?
slide.text: DALI (Distributed Active Logic-based Interactive) is an agent-oriented programming language originally developed on SICStus Prolog.

bullets.b: **Logic-based agents** — each agent is a Prolog program with reactive and proactive capabilities
**BDI-inspired** — agents have Beliefs, react to events, pursue Goals
**Event-driven** — external events (:>), internal events, condition-action rules
**Communication** — agents send messages using Linda-like coordination
**Past memory** — agents remember what happened and reason about history
**FIPA-ACL support** — standard agent communication language
**Original implementation** — ~20 source files, SICStus Prolog, TCP sockets, Python UI

slide.title: From DALI to DALI2
slide.text: DALI2 is the evolution of DALI — same language, modern architecture.

bullets.b: **Same syntax** — identical operators (:>, :<, ~/, </, ?/, :~) and suffixes (E, I, A, N, P)
**SWI-Prolog** — free, open-source, cross-platform (replaces SICStus)
**8 source files** — down from ~20 in the original
**Single-file multi-agent** — all agents in one .pl file
**Redis communication** — replaces TCP sockets with pub/sub star topology
**Integrated web UI** — dashboard, log viewer, agent inspector (replaces separate Python UI)
**Docker-ready** — one command to run everything
**New features** — periodic tasks, condition monitors, helpers, AI Oracle, blackboard
**Identical DALI syntax** — no prefix needed, same operators and suffixes
**Process-per-agent** — each agent runs as a separate OS process (swipl)
**Redis star topology** — LINDA channel (messages), LOGS channel (monitoring), BB SET (blackboard)
**Single-file multi-agent** — :- agent(name). sets context for subsequent rules
**Full DALI feature set** — reactive rules, internal events, goals, constraints, tell/told
**Integrated web UI** — real-time dashboard, event sender, belief inspector
**AI Oracle** — connect to any LLM via OpenRouter (GPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.)
**LAN-ready** — distributed deployment across multiple machines
**Docker support** — single command setup with Redis included

slide.title: Prerequisites
slide.text: Two ways to run DALI2:

bullets.b: **With Docker (recommended)** — only Docker Desktop needed; Redis starts automatically
**Without Docker** — requires SWI-Prolog (>= 9.0) and Redis (>= 6.0)
**Redis** — must be running before starting DALI2; default: localhost:6379
**Environment variables** — REDIS_HOST and REDIS_PORT to override defaults
**Optional** — OPENROUTER_API_KEY for AI Oracle integration

slide.title: Quick Start
slide.text: Start DALI2 with Docker or SWI-Prolog, then open http://localhost:8080
slide.code:
%% With Docker (recommended) — Redis starts automatically docker compose up --build
%% Choose a different agent file AGENT_FILE=examples/emergency.pl docker compose up --build
%% Without Docker — start Redis first, then: redis-server swipl -l src/server.pl -g main -- 8080 examples/agriculture.pl
%% Windows run.bat

slide.title: Architecture
slide.subtitle: Redis Star Topology · Process-per-Agent · Deployment Modes

slide.title: Redis Star Topology
slide.text: All agents communicate through a central Redis instance. No direct agent-to-agent connections.

slide.title: Three Communication Channels
slide.text: Redis provides three distinct channels for different purposes:

bullets.b: **LINDA channel** — pub/sub for inter-agent messages. Format: TO:CONTENT:FROM. All agents subscribe. TO=* for broadcast.
**LOGS channel** — pub/sub for monitoring. Agents publish log entries; external tools can subscribe.
**BB (Blackboard)** — Redis SET for shared data. Replaces DALI's Linda tuple space. Agents use bb_read/bb_write/bb_remove.

slide.title: Process-per-Agent Model
slide.text: Each agent runs as a separate OS process — true parallelism and isolation.

bullets.b: **One swipl process per agent** — the master server spawns each agent as an independent process
**Cycle-based execution** — each agent has its own event loop with configurable cycle duration
**Independent failure** — if one agent crashes, others continue running
**Redis decoupling** — agents don't know each other's location; all routing is via Redis
**Scalable** — add agents by adding :- agent(name). declarations to the .pl file
**No shared memory** — all inter-agent state goes through Redis (messages or blackboard)

slide.title: Deployment Modes
slide.text: Same code works in all three modes — just change how processes are started.

bullets.b: **Mode A: All-in-one** — one Redis, one server, all agents in one terminal. Simplest setup.
**Mode B: Multi-terminal** — multiple server instances on the same machine share one Redis. Each starts a subset of agents with --agents flag.
**Mode C: Multi-machine** — same as Mode B but across LAN. All nodes point REDIS_HOST to the Redis machine.
**No code changes** — agents communicate through Redis regardless of deployment mode
**Cluster panel** — web UI shows all agents across all nodes

slide.title: Distributed Deployment (Mode C)
slide.text: Multiple machines on the same LAN, all pointing to one Redis instance.

slide.title: Deployment Commands
slide.text: Examples for each deployment mode:
slide.code:
%% Mode A: All-in-one (simplest) docker compose up --build
%% Mode B: Multi-terminal (same machine, shared Redis) %% Terminal 1 — sensors node (port 8081) swipl -l src/server.pl -g main -- 8081 examples/agriculture.pl \ --name sensors --agents soil_sensor,weather_monitor,logger
%% Terminal 2 — advisors node (port 8082) swipl -l src/server.pl -g main -- 8082 examples/agriculture.pl \ --name advisors --agents crop_advisor,irrigation_controller,farmer_agent
%% Mode C: Multi-machine — set REDIS_HOST REDIS_HOST=192.168.1.10 swipl -l src/server.pl -g main -- 8081 \ examples/agriculture.pl --name sensors --agents soil_sensor,logger

slide.title: Agent Declaration
slide.text: Every agent is declared with :- agent(Name, Options). All subsequent rules belong to that agent.
slide.code:
%% Agent with options :- agent(my_agent, [cycle(1)]).
%% Agent without options (defaults to cycle(1)) :- agent(simple_agent).
%% Multiple agents in one file :- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]). %% ... sensor rules here ...
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]). %% ... coordinator rules here ...
%% Options: %% cycle(N) — cycle duration in seconds (default: 1)

slide.title: Suffixes — Event and Predicate Types
slide.text: DALI2 uses single-letter suffixes to classify predicates. Identical to original DALI.

row.cell: React to incoming messages or injected events
Check if event exists in past memory

slide.title: Operators — Rule Types
slide.text: DALI2 operators define different kinds of rules. All identical to original DALI.

row.cell: React to external or internal events
Edge-triggered: fires once when cond becomes true
Fire when past events match, then consume them
Fire only if action was NOT done
Fire only if action WAS done

slide.title: Beliefs
slide.text: Beliefs are facts the agent holds true. They can be declared statically and modified at runtime.
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Static beliefs (declared in agent file) believes(target_temp(22)). believes(current_temp(20)). believes(mode(idle)).
%% Runtime operations (used in rule bodies): %% assert_belief(Fact) — add a belief %% retract_belief(Fact) — remove a belief %% believes(Fact) — check if a belief exists (ontology-aware)
%% Example in a rule body: update_tempE(NewT) :> retract_belief(current_temp(_)), assert_belief(current_temp(NewT)), log("Temperature updated to ~w", [NewT]).

slide.title: External Events (E + :>)
slide.text: React to incoming messages from other agents or injected events. The E suffix is stripped when matching.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% React to a simple event readingE(Value) :> log("Sensor reading: ~w", [Value]), send(analyzer, data(Value)).
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(1)]).
%% React with pattern matching alarmE(Type, Location) :> log("Alarm: ~w at ~w", [Type, Location]), assert_belief(active_alarm(Type, Location)), send(responder, respond(Location, Type)).

slide.title: Communication: send and messageA
slide.text: Two equivalent ways to send messages — DALI2 style (send) and classic DALI style (messageA).
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% DALI2 style — concise readingE(Value) :> send(analyzer, data(Value)), broadcast(heartbeat(sensor)).
%% Classic DALI style — identical behavior readingE(Value) :> messageA(analyzer, send_message(data(Value), sensor)).
%% Other communication predicates: %% send(Agent, Content) — send to one agent %% broadcast(Content) — send to all agents %% from(Sender) — get sender of current message %% reply_to(Content) — reply to current message sender

slide.title: Actions (A Suffix)
slide.text: Named, reusable actions. Actions are recorded in past memory as did(Action).
slide.code:
:- agent(worker, [cycle(2)]).
%% Define an action (A suffix) analyzeA(Data) :- log("Executing analysis: ~w", [Data]), assert_belief(analysis_complete(Data)), send(coordinator, inform(analysis_result(Data), complete)).
%% Call the action from a rule body using do/1 request_analysisE(Data) :> do(analyze(Data)).
%% After execution, has_past(did(analyze(Data))) is true %% This enables export past rules (?/) to check if action was done

slide.title: Logging
slide.text: Agents log messages to the LOGS Redis channel. Visible in the web UI event log.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% Simple log message readingE(Value) :> log("Sensor reading received"), log("Value: ~w", [Value]).
%% Format syntax (same as Prolog format/2) %% ~w — write term %% ~d — write integer %% ~a — write atom %% ~f — write float
alarmE(Type, Loc) :> log("ALARM: type=~w location=~w", [Type, Loc]).

slide.title: Internal Events (I + :>)
slide.text: Proactive events that fire automatically based on timing and conditions. Defined by handler + configuration.
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Handler: what to do when the internal event fires temp_checkI :> believes(current_temp(T)), log("Temperature check, current=~w", [T]).
%% Configuration: when and how often to fire %% internal_event(Event, Period, Repetition, StartCond, StopCond). internal_event(temp_check, 5, forever, true, forever).
%% Period=5 — fire every 5 seconds %% forever — repeat indefinitely %% true — start immediately (no guard condition) %% forever — never stop

slide.title: internal_event/5 — Configuration
slide.text: Each parameter controls a different aspect of the internal event's lifecycle.

row.cell: How many times / when to reset counter
Guard: must be true before event fires
Stop condition: event stops when true

slide.title: Internal Event Options
slide.text: Advanced options for fine-grained control over internal event behavior.
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Fire at most 3 times, reset when current_temp changes startup_diagnosticI :> log("Startup diagnostic"). internal_event(startup_diagnostic, 0, change([current_temp(_)]), true, forever).
%% Fire only when mode is cooling (trigger/start condition) cooling_monitorI :> believes(current_temp(T)), log("Monitoring cooling, temp: ~w", [T]). internal_event(cooling_monitor, 0, forever, believes(mode(cooling)), forever).
%% Fire only during work hours work_hours_checkI :> log("Work hours system check"). internal_event(work_hours_check, 10, forever, true, in_date(time(9,0), time(17,0))).

slide.title: Periodic Tasks (every)
slide.text: Simple periodic execution at fixed intervals. No conditional options — just fires every N seconds. [NEW in DALI2]
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% Simple periodic log (one-liner) every(10, log("Heartbeat")).
%% Periodic with body every(30) :- log("Checking system health"), send(dashboard, health_check).
%% Difference from internal events: %% every — simple timer, no conditions %% internal — full lifecycle (start/stop/change/trigger conditions)

slide.title: Condition Monitors (when)
slide.text: Check a condition every cycle. Level-triggered: fires every cycle while condition holds. [NEW in DALI2]
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Fire every cycle while temperature is above 30 when(believes(temperature(T)), T > 30) :- send(ac_controller, cool_down).
:- agent(alarm, [cycle(1)]).
%% Check a past event condition when(has_past(intrusion_detected)) :- send(security, alert).
%% Supports: believes(...), has_past(...), learned(...), %% bb_read(...), arithmetic comparisons

slide.title: Condition-Action Rules (:<)
slide.text: Edge-triggered: fires exactly once when condition transitions from false to true. Does not fire again until condition becomes false and then true again.
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Fire once when cooling mode activates believes(mode(cooling)) :< ( log("Cooling mode just activated"), send(logger, log_event(mode_change, thermostat, cooling)) ).
:- agent(robot, [cycle(1)]).
%% Fire once when battery becomes low believes(battery_level(L)), L < 20 :< ( log("Battery low! Requesting charge."), send(charger, request_charge) ).

slide.title: when vs :< — Level vs Edge Triggered
slide.text: Both check conditions every cycle, but they fire differently:

bullets.b: **when (level-triggered)** — fires EVERY CYCLE while the condition is true. Good for continuous monitoring.
**:< (edge-triggered)** — fires ONCE when the condition becomes true. Waits for false-to-true transition before firing again.
**Example:** temperature goes above 30 and stays there for 10 cycles
**when** — fires 10 times (once per cycle)
**:<** — fires 1 time (on the first cycle where T > 30)
**Use when** for continuous reactions (keep sending alerts while hot)
**Use :<** for one-shot reactions (notify once when it becomes hot)

slide.title: Present Events (N Suffix)
slide.text: Monitor the environment every cycle. Semantically represents observations from the agent's world.
slide.code:
:- agent(robot, [cycle(1)]).
%% React to observable state (N suffix) obstacle_aheadN :- do(turn_around).
%% The N suffix condition is evaluated each cycle %% If the condition is true, the body fires %% Similar to 'when' but semantically different: %% N = environment observation %% when = internal condition check

slide.title: Multi-Events (Conjunction)
slide.text: Fire when ALL listed events have occurred in the agent's past memory.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% Fire when BOTH sensor_data AND alert have been received sensor_dataE(_), alertE(_, _) :> log("Both sensor data and alert received!"), send(logger, log_event(combined_alert, coordinator)).
%% How it works: %% 1. Agent receives sensor_data(85) — stored in past %% 2. Agent receives alert(fire, building) — stored in past %% 3. Next cycle: engine checks past for both patterns %% 4. Both found -> body fires %% 5. Resets if any event is removed from past

slide.title: Constraints (:~)
slide.text: Invariant conditions checked every cycle. If violated (condition is false), the handler fires.
slide.code:
:- agent(thermostat, [cycle(2)]).
%% Safety constraint: temperature must stay below 50 :~ (believes(current_temp(T)), T < 50) :- log("CONSTRAINT VIOLATED: Temp ~w exceeds limit!", [T]), send(coordinator, emergency(overheating, T)).
:- agent(server, [cycle(1)]).
%% Simple constraint (log-only, no handler body) :~ believes(config_loaded).
%% With handler: fires when condition is FALSE %% Without handler: logs a warning when violated

slide.title: Goals and Memory
slide.subtitle: Achieve Goals · Test Goals · Residue · Past Events · Export Past

slide.title: Achieve Goals (obt_goal)
slide.text: Keep trying a plan every cycle until the goal condition is met.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(2)]).
%% Keep sending calibration requests until calibrated obt_goal(believes(calibrated(true))) :- log("Attempting calibration..."), send(coordinator, calibration_request).
%% Lifecycle: %% 1. Each cycle: check if believes(calibrated(true)) %% 2. If NO -> execute plan (send calibration_request) %% 3. If YES -> mark as "achieved", stop executing %% 4. Use reset_goal(Goal) to allow re-attempt

slide.title: Test Goals (test_goal)
slide.text: Try the plan once, then check if the goal succeeded. Does not retry.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% Test if we received at least one alert test_goal(believes(alerts_received(N)), N > 0) :- log("Testing if any alerts received...").
%% Lifecycle: %% 1. Execute plan ONCE %% 2. Check goal condition %% 3. Record result: succeeded / failed / error %% 4. Will NOT retry (unlike obt_goal) %% %% Difference from obt_goal: %% obt_goal — persistent, retries until achieved %% test_goal — one-shot, records result

slide.title: Residue Goals
slide.text: When achieve(Goal) is called but the goal is not yet satisfiable, it becomes a residue goal — automatically retried each cycle.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% The achieve call queues the goal as residue start_residue_testE :> log("Starting residue goal test..."), achieve(has_past(residue_resolved)).
%% Lifecycle: %% 1. achieve(Goal) called — Goal not yet true %% 2. Goal queued as RESIDUE %% 3. Each cycle: re-check all residue goals %% 4. When condition becomes true -> achieved, removed from residue %% %% DALI equivalent: tenta_residuo(goal)

slide.title: Past Event Lifetime
slide.text: Control how long past events are kept in memory. Expired events move to the remember tier.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(2)]).
%% past_event(Pattern, Duration) %% Sensor readings expire after 30 seconds past_event(sensor_data(_), 30).
%% Alarms never expire past_event(alarm(_), forever).
%% Check past in rule bodies: %% has_past(Event) — is event in past memory? %% has_past(Event, Time) — with timestamp %% evp(Event) — DALI-style past check %% eventP(Args) — DALI-style with P suffix

slide.title: Remember Events
slide.text: Expired past events move to a "remember" tier. Control remember lifetime and limits.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(2)]).
%% Past expires after 30s, then remembered for 5 minutes past_event(sensor_data(_), 30). remember_event(sensor_data(_), 300).
%% Keep only the last 10 remembered events remember_event_mod(sensor_data(_), number(10), last). %% number(N) — max entries %% last — keep newest | first — keep oldest
%% Check remembered events: %% has_remember(Event) %% has_remember(Event, Time)

slide.title: Export Past — ~/ Operator
slide.text: Fire when past event patterns match, then CONSUME (delete) the matched past events.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% When both alert and sensor_data in past, consume and react send(logger, log_event(combined, [Type, Value])) ~/ alert(Type, _), sensor_data(Value).
%% After firing: %% - alert(Type, _) REMOVED from past %% - sensor_data(Value) REMOVED from past %% - Rule cannot fire again with same events %% - New events can trigger it again

slide.title: Export Past — </ and ?/ Operators
slide.text: Conditional export past based on whether an action was or was not done.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% </ — Fire only if action was NOT done %% Warn if backup was NOT done but critical data exists log("Backup NOT done! critical_data needs attention!") </ critical_data(_).
%% ?/ — Fire only if action WAS done %% Only react if we already dispatched a response send(logger, log_event(followup)) ?/ alarm(fire, _), evacuated(_).
%% Summary: %% ~/ — fire when past events match (unconditional) %% </ — fire when past events match AND action NOT done %% ?/ — fire when past events match AND action WAS done

slide.title: Inter-Agent Communication
slide.subtitle: Tell/Told Filtering · Priority Queues · FIPA Messages · Proposals

slide.title: Tell/Told Filtering
slide.text: Control which messages an agent can send (tell) and receive (told). Mirrors DALI's communication.con.
slide.code:
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% TOLD — receive filter (what I accept, with priority) told(_, emergency(_, _), 200) :- true. %% highest priority told(_, alert(_, _), 100) :- true. told(_, sensor_data(_), 30) :- true. told(_, calibration_request, 10) :- true. %% lowest priority
%% TELL — send filter (what I'm allowed to send) tell(_, _, calibration_done) :- true. tell(_, _, log_event(_, _, _)) :- true.
%% If agent has told rules: only matching messages accepted %% If agent has NO told rules: all messages accepted %% Same for tell rules

slide.title: Priority Queue
slide.text: When an agent has told rules with numeric priorities, incoming messages are sorted before processing.

bullets.b: **Priority values** — the third argument of told/3 is a numeric priority
**Highest first** — messages with higher priority are processed first in each cycle
**Example:** emergency(200) is processed before sensor_data(30)
**Multiple messages per cycle** — all pending messages are sorted, then processed in order
**Rejected messages** — messages not matching any told pattern are rejected with a log entry
**AI Oracle filtering** — tell/told rules also apply to AI queries and responses

slide.title: FIPA Message Types
slide.text: DALI2 supports FIPA-ACL message types for structured inter-agent communication.

slide.title: FIPA Examples
slide.text: Using FIPA message types for structured agent interaction.
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% Confirm a fact to another agent measurement_completeE(Data) :> send(coordinator, confirm(measurement(Data))).
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% Query another agent's beliefs need_statusE :> send(sensor, query_ref(status(_))).
%% Handle the auto-response from query_ref informE(query_ref(Q), values(V)) :> log("Query ~w returned: ~w", [Q, V]).
%% Check confirmed facts: has_confirmed(Fact)

slide.title: Action Proposals (on_proposal)
slide.text: Negotiation between agents using FIPA propose/accept/reject protocol.
slide.code:
:- agent(worker, [cycle(2)]).
%% Handle incoming proposals on_proposal(analyze(Data)) :- believes(skill(data_analysis)), from(Sender), log("Accepting analyze(~w) from ~w", [Data, Sender]), accept_proposal(Sender, analyze(Data)), do(analyze(Data)).
on_proposal(impossible_task) :- from(Sender), reject_proposal(Sender, impossible_task).
:- agent(coordinator, [cycle(2)]).
%% Send a proposal request_analysisE(Data) :> send(worker, propose(analyze(Data))).

slide.title: Advanced Features
slide.subtitle: Ontology · Learning · Helpers · Blackboard · AI Oracle

slide.title: Ontology Declarations
slide.text: Define semantic equivalences. Makes event matching and belief checking ontology-aware.
slide.code:
:- agent(logger, [cycle(2)]).
%% Inline ontology declarations ontology(same_as(log_event, log_entry)). ontology(eq_property(temperature, temp)). ontology(eq_class(sensor, detector)). ontology(symmetric(related_to)).
%% Effects: %% believes(warm(room1)) matches hot(room1) if same_as(hot,warm) %% readingE(temp(30)) matches reading(temperature(30)) %% nearE(a,b) matches near(b,a) if symmetric(near)
%% External ontology file ontology_file('path/to/ontology.pl').
%% Explicit check in rule body: onto_match(T1, T2)

slide.title: Learning Rules (learn_from)
slide.text: Agents learn from experience. Triggered when matching events occur. [NEW in DALI2]
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(2)]).
%% Learn that high readings indicate overheating learn_from(read_temp(T), overheating) :- T > 80.
%% Learn that low readings are normal learn_from(read_temp(T), normal) :- T =< 80.
%% Use learned knowledge in rules: read_tempE(T) :> ( learned(read_temp(_), overheating) -> log("WARNING: Previously learned overheating!"), send(coordinator, alert(repeated_overheating, T)) ; true ).
%% Predicates: learn(P,O), learned(P,O), forget(P)

slide.title: Helpers
slide.text: Utility predicates that can be called from rule bodies. [NEW in DALI2]
slide.code:
:- agent(logger, [cycle(2)]).
%% Define a helper helper(count_logs) :- findall(_, believes(logged(_, _)), Logs), length(Logs, N), log("Total log entries: ~w", [N]).
%% Call helper from a rule body log_eventE(Type, Source) :> assert_belief(logged(Type, Source)), helper(count_logs).
%% Helpers are agent-local utility predicates %% They can use all DSL predicates (believes, send, log, etc.)

slide.title: Blackboard Operations
slide.text: Shared blackboard via Redis SET. All agents can read/write tuples. [NEW in DALI2]
slide.code:
:- agent(sensor, [cycle(1)]).
%% Write to shared blackboard readingE(Value) :> bb_write(env_data(temperature, Value)), log("Wrote temperature to blackboard").
:- agent(monitor, [cycle(2)]).
%% Read from blackboard (non-destructive) when(bb_read(env_data(temperature, T)), T > 40) :- log("Blackboard says temperature is ~w!", [T]).
%% Remove from blackboard clearE :> bb_remove(env_data(temperature, _)).

slide.title: AI Oracle
slide.text: Connect to any LLM via OpenRouter. Agents send context, receive Prolog facts back.

left.b: **Configuration:** set OPENROUTER_API_KEY environment variable, web UI, or POST /api/ai/key
**Optional:** if no key, ai_available fails and ask_ai returns suggestion(no_ai_available)
**Models:** free (Gemini, Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek) and paid (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini Pro)
**Filtered:** tell/told rules apply to AI queries and responses

right.code:
:- agent(advisor, [cycle(2)]).
analyzeE(Data) :> ( ai_available -> ask_ai(analyze(Data), Advice), log("AI: ~w", [Advice]), send(coord, ai_rec(Advice)) ; log("AI not available") ).
%% ask_ai(Context, Result) %% ask_ai(Context, Prompt, Result)

slide.title: Agent Cycle — 13 Steps
slide.text: Each agent runs a cycle-based event loop. All 13 steps execute in order every cycle.
slide.diagram: +-------------------------------------------+ | Agent Cycle | +-------------------------------------------+ | 1. Process messages (priority queue) | | 2. Process injected events | | 3. Process internals (interval/change) | | 4. Process periodic tasks | | 5. Process condition monitors | | 6. Process condition-actions | | 7. Process present events | | 8. Process multi-events | | 9. Process past reactions (export past) | | 10. Check constraints | | 11. Process goals + residue goals | | 12. Clean up expired past events | | 13. Sleep for cycle duration | | +--- repeat ---+ | +-------------------------------------------+

slide.title: Past Memory Types
slide.text: Every event is recorded in past memory with a timestamp and source type.

bullets.b: **received(Content, From)** — external message received from another agent
**injected(Event)** — event injected via REST API or web UI
**internal(Event)** — internal event that fired
**did(Action)** — action that was executed via do/1
**goal_achieved(Goal)** — goal that was successfully achieved
**confirmed(Fact)** — fact confirmed via FIPA confirm message
Past events have configurable lifetimes (past_event/2)
Expired past events move to the remember tier (remember_event/2)

slide.title: DSL Predicates — Communication and Beliefs
slide.text: Predicates available inside rule bodies:

row.cell: Send message to another agent (filtered by tell/told)

slide.title: DSL Predicates — Past, Actions, Learning, Goals
slide.text: More predicates available inside rule bodies:

row.cell: Check if event is in past memory
Check if event is in remember tier
Check if fact was confirmed via FIPA
Execute a named action (recorded as did(Action))

slide.title: Examples
slide.subtitle: Smart Agriculture · Emergency Response · Feature Showcase · Distributed

slide.title: Example: Smart Agriculture
slide.text: File: examples/agriculture.pl — Precision agriculture with 6 agents.

left.b: **soil_sensor** — receives soil readings, validates via internal events

right.code: %% Test: send to soil_sensor read_soil(25, 6.5, north_field) %% Flow: %% sensor -> crop_advisor: soil_report %% advisor: low moisture -> irrigate %% advisor -> irrigation: irrigate(field) %% advisor -> farmer: advisory %% irrigation -> farmer: status

slide.title: Example: Emergency Response
slide.text: File: examples/emergency.pl — 9-agent emergency response system.

left.b: **sensor** — detects events, validates alarms (real vs false) via internal events
**coordinator** — multi-step dispatch: AI analysis, waits for equipment, tracks completion

right.code: %% Test: send to sensor sense(fire, building_a) %% Flow: %% sensor validates -> alarm %% coord -> evac + comm + mgr %% mgr: fire -> firetruck %% comm -> mary + john %% evac -> coord: evacuated %% coord: equip+loc -> responder %% responder -> coord: responded %% coord: RESOLVED

slide.title: Example: Feature Showcase
slide.text: File: examples/showcase.pl — Demonstrates ALL 32 DALI2 features in one file.

bullets.b: **thermostat** — internal events (interval, change, trigger, between), constraints, condition-action
**sensor** — periodic tasks, present events, learning, blackboard, past lifetime, goals
**coordinator** — tell/told, FIPA messages, multi-events, goals, residue, export past, AI oracle
**logger** — ontology (inline + external file), helpers, condition monitors
**worker** — action proposals (on_proposal), FIPA responses, actions, told rules
**10-step test sequence** — from basic events to FIPA proposals to residue goals

slide.title: Example: Distributed Emergency
slide.text: Two separate DALI2 nodes communicating via shared Redis (star topology).

left.b: Cross-node communication via Redis LINDA channel
Cluster panel shows all agents across nodes

right.code:
%% Start with Docker Compose docker compose \ -f docker-compose.distributed.yml \ up --build
%% Or manually (3 terminals): %% T1: Redis docker run -d -p 6379:6379 \ redis:7-alpine %% T2: Sensors (port 8080) swipl -l src/server.pl -g main \ -- 8080 emergency_sensors.pl %% T3: Responders (port 8081) swipl -l src/server.pl -g main \ -- 8081 emergency_responders.pl

slide.title: DALI vs DALI2 — Key Differences
slide.text: DALI2 is fully backward compatible with DALI syntax. Key architectural differences:

slide.title: DALI vs DALI2 — New Features
slide.text: Features available in DALI2 that have no DALI equivalent:

slide.title: Web UI and REST API
slide.text: Integrated web interface at http://localhost:8080 with full REST API.

bullets.b: **Agent list** — local and remote agents with running/stopped status
**Event log** — real-time log with filtering by agent
**Send events** — inject events into any agent from the browser
**Agent details** — beliefs, past events, goals, start/stop controls
**Blackboard viewer** — current shared blackboard state
**Source editor** — edit and hot-reload agent definitions (double-click logo)
**Cluster panel** — view all agents across all nodes in the Redis cluster
**AI Oracle panel** — configure API key, model, and test AI queries
**REST API** — full programmatic access: /api/agents, /api/send, /api/beliefs, /api/logs, etc.

slide.title: REST API Reference
slide.text: All endpoints available at http://localhost:8080/api/

slide.title: Thank You
slide.subtitle:
DALI2 — Multi-Agent System Framework University of L'Aquila — DISIM
Documentation: RULES.md (language reference) · EXAMPLES.md (examples guide) Source: github.com/AAAI-DISIM-UnivAQ/DALI2
